L157: #  How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them 
L158: 
L159: ##  ... and what happens when people can't turn them off. 
L160: 
L161: Posted April 25, 2023 | 【66† Reviewed by Michelle Quirk 】 
L162: 
L163:   * 【67† Share †facebook.com】 
L164:   * 【68† Tweet †twitter.com】 
L165:   * 【69† Share †www.linkedin.com】 
L166:   * [Image 2: Share via Email]Email
L167: 
L168: THE BASICS
L169: 
L170:   * 【65†What Is a Career】 
L171:   * 【70†Find a career counselor near me】 
L172: 
L173: ### Key points
L174: 
L175:   * About 30 to 50 percent of people regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. 
L176:   * Inner monologues have a function in language development and in information and memory processing.
L177:   * This phenomenon demonstrates a rich diversity of experience in what we deem to be "normal" thought lives.
L178: 
L179: Flashback to college: I’m sitting in the dining hall with my best friend Dave, and a student enters in a firefighter outfit. Assuming (and probably correctly) that this man is part of the arts/creative “Western campus program,” a woman at our table exclaims, “Those Western people….! They just think all of the time!” My friend and I give each other the side-eye, and he later asks incredulously, “Really? Is there any other option?”
L180: 
L181: It turns out there is, at least in the sense of having an inner conversation in unspoken words. About 30 to 50 percent of people, according to psychologist Russell Hurlburt’s research, regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. Inner or "private" speech is something most of us likely did as very young people seeking to develop our language skills, and later as a way of rehearsing information to successfully encode and retain working 【71†memory】. So it’s clearly functional and not a sign of mental disorder.
L182: 
L183: To get a read on whether you have inner monologues, try listening in and noting an internal voice or intrusive thoughts during 【72†meditation】. 【73†Mindful】 practice provides tremendous insights into whether you have and how often you have inner monologues. But what about the 50 to 70 percent of folks who don’t have or infrequently have words in their heads?
L184: 
L185: ## Visual Imagery
L186: 
L187: There are different theories, but the simplest (and least condescending/least pejorative) view of folks who don’t regularly have inner monologues is that many of them are processing information and prepping for tasks using visual imagery rather than words. That is, they see images, such as a to-do list, rather than thinking about or hearing the words for the items on the list. I found this explanation helpful because I think in terms of words, visual images, and music all day long, and it’s easy to perspective take on folks who are a bit “quiet” in there by relating to their use of images or their playing back a song in their heads, things I do, too. It’s helpful to think about their inner experience in visual terms, as while it might be quiet as the occasional cricket when it comes to words, it’s not a total void or vacuum in there.
L188: 
L189: ## Too Much of a Good Thing
L190: 
L191: And before those of us start to feel wonderful about our rich inner monologues, it’s important to remember that too much of a good thing is possible. Inner monologues can become a little like King Midas’s golden touch when we can’t turn them off. For instance, 【20†anxious】 minds continuously scan for and entertain intrusive thoughts, and rumination on these can lead to brooding, and brooding can give itself over to highly critical talk about self and others. In the particular instance of an inner critic, a lack of inner monologue sounds like a quiet, peaceful reprieve from constant chatting and potentially corrosive 【74†self-talk】.
L192: 
L193: I know people who have internal dialogues and “think in words all of the time.” I know a person who has internal monologues and external dialogues with himself, engaging in rich conversations that can lead in unexpected, even challenging, directions. I can imagine that sounds weird to folks who have only inner monologues or those who have no inner monologues at all. But as Vulcan 【75†philosophy】 states, “Infinite diversity in infinite combinations,” which is another way of saying “Different strokes for different folks,” and empathy, 【76†perspective-taking】, and imagining what others’ experiences are like—and what it’s like inside their heads—are worthwhile, growth-enhancing, and humanizing exercises.
L194: 
L195: Inner monologues can represent a rich, deep, “pristine” experience (Hurlburt et al., 2016) for some, as long as they don’t get out of hand, and as long as external monologues/dialogues don’t freak out your friends, family members, and co-workers.

* About 30 to 50 percent of people regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. 
  * Inner monologues have a function in language development and in information and memory processing.
  * This phenomenon demonstrates a rich diversity of experience in what we deem to be "normal" thought lives.
content.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today

metadata_list.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today
metadata_list.text:
* About 30 to 50 percent of people regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. 
  * Inner monologues have a function in language development and in information and memory processing.
  * This phenomenon demonstrates a rich diversity of experience in what we deem to be "normal" thought lives.

content.text: It turns out there is, at least in the sense of having an inner conversation in unspoken words. About 30 to 50 percent of people, according to psychologist Russell Hurlburt’s research, regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. Inner or "private" speech is something most of us likely did as very young people seeking to develop our language skills, and later as a way of rehearsing information to successfully encode and retain working 【71†memory】. So it’s clearly functional and not a sign of mental disorder.
content.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today

metadata_list.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today
metadata_list.text: It turns out there is, at least in the sense of having an inner conversation in unspoken words. About 30 to 50 percent of people, according to psychologist Russell Hurlburt’s research, regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. Inner or "private" speech is something most of us likely did as very young people seeking to develop our language skills, and later as a way of rehearsing information to successfully encode and retain working 【71†memory】. So it’s clearly functional and not a sign of mental disorder.

content.text: There are different theories, but the simplest (and least condescending/least pejorative) view of folks who don’t regularly have inner monologues is that many of them are processing information and prepping for tasks using visual imagery rather than words. That is, they see images, such as a to-do list, rather than thinking about or hearing the words for the items on the list. I found this explanation helpful because I think in terms of words, visual images, and music all day long, and it’s easy to perspective take on folks who are a bit “quiet” in there by relating to their use of images or their playing back a song in their heads, things I do, too. It’s helpful to think about their inner experience in visual terms, as while it might be quiet as the occasional cricket when it comes to words, it’s not a total void or vacuum in there.

Do you always have an inner monologue? Can you imagine not having one? Or maybe you've never had an inner monologue and wondered what it would be like? Psychologists are beginning to study that voice inside your head— and it's much more complicated than you might think. Join Hank Green for a fascinating episode of SciShow, all about your inner voice.
content.title: Why some people don't have an inner monologue | TED-Ed

metadata_list.title: Why some people don't have an inner monologue | TED-Ed
metadata_list.text: Do you always have an inner monologue? Can you imagine not having one? Or maybe you've never had an inner monologue and wondered what it would be like? Psychologists are beginning to study that voice inside your head— and it's much more complicated than you might think. Join Hank Green for a fascinating episode of SciShow, all about your inner voice.

parts:
The question of whether all people have an inner monologue is a fascinating and complex area of psychological study. The research in this field suggests a rich diversity in the way people think and process information internally.

According to psychologist Russell Hurlburt, about 30 to 50 percent of people regularly engage in internal monologues, where they think to themselves in unspoken words. This inner or "private" speech is not only common but also plays a crucial role in the development of language skills and in the processing of information and memory. Most people likely engaged in this form of speech as young children while developing their language skills, and later as a means of rehearsing information to improve memory retention【13†source】.

However, not everyone experiences an inner monologue in the same way. Some individuals may process information and prepare for tasks using visual imagery rather than words. For example, instead of thinking about or hearing the words for items on a to-do list, they might visualize the list or see images related to the tasks. This suggests that the inner experience of these individuals is more visually oriented, as opposed to being dominated by verbal thoughts【14†source】.

Overall, the study of inner monologues is still evolving, and psychologists are continuing to explore this internal voice. It's a complex subject, as the existence and nature of the inner monologue can vary greatly from person to person【28†source】. 

These studies highlight the diversity in cognitive processing among individuals, indicating that the presence and nature of an inner monologue can differ widely, reflecting the varied ways in which people think and experience the world.

metadata.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today
metadata.text: It turns out there is, at least in the sense of having an inner conversation in unspoken words. About 30 to 50 percent of people, according to psychologist Russell Hurlburt’s research, regularly think to themselves in internal monologues. Inner or "private" speech is something most of us likely did as very young people seeking to develop our language skills, and later as a way of rehearsing information to successfully encode and retain working 【71†memory】. So it’s clearly functional and not a sign of mental disorder.

metadata.title: How Inner Monologues Work, and Who Has Them | Psychology Today
metadata.text: There are different theories, but the simplest (and least condescending/least pejorative) view of folks who don’t regularly have inner monologues is that many of them are processing information and prepping for tasks using visual imagery rather than words. That is, they see images, such as a to-do list, rather than thinking about or hearing the words for the items on the list. I found this explanation helpful because I think in terms of words, visual images, and music all day long, and it’s easy to perspective take on folks who are a bit “quiet” in there by relating to their use of images or their playing back a song in their heads, things I do, too. It’s helpful to think about their inner experience in visual terms, as while it might be quiet as the occasional cricket when it comes to words, it’s not a total void or vacuum in there.

content_references.snippet: Do you always have an inner monologue? Can you imagine not having one? Or maybe you've never had an inner monologue and wondered what it would be like? Psychologists are beginning to study that voice inside your head— and it's much more complicated than you might think. Join Hank Green for a fascinating episode of SciShow, all about your inner voice.

parts: Do some people not have an inner experience at all?

The concept of not having any inner experience at all is quite intriguing. Generally, it's not that some people lack an inner experience entirely, but rather, their inner experiences may manifest differently. While many people have an inner monologue or think in words, others might have inner experiences that are more visual or abstract, involving images or sensory experiences rather than verbal thoughts.

The idea of an entirely absent inner experience is challenging to confirm, as inner experiences are inherently subjective and can vary greatly among individuals. The diversity in cognitive processing and the subjective nature of consciousness make it unlikely that a person would have no inner experience whatsoever. Instead, their inner world might be organized and experienced in a way that is fundamentally different from the more commonly discussed verbal or visual thought processes.
